blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Three days later we went to the cardiologist. The office had beige walls. That's one of the clearest things I remember — not the doctor's face, not what he said at first. The walls. This particular beige that was supposed to be calming and wasn't.
The wheel carries them upward. The sky is wide from up here.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: My mom sat next to me and she had her hand on my knee. I was so aware of her hand. The weight of it. I kept thinking she was trying to hold me down. Or hold herself down. I still don't know which.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: The doctor was talking. I could hear the words. They just weren't — they weren't arriving yet. They were sounds in a row. I was looking at the wall, and my mom's hand, and this paper covering on the exam table that made a noise every time I shifted. And I kept shifting. And it kept making the noise.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: A structural condition. That's the language. My heart — the architecture of it — has a flaw in how it was built. It has to work harder than it should. And over time, it gets tired.
